title: Glutamate Receptor Dysfunction and Schizophrenia
abstract:
Glutamate Receptor Dysfunction and Schizophrenia
John W. Olney, MD, Nuri B. Farber, MD
In
this article, we advance a unified hypothesis pertaining to combined dysfunction of do-
pamine and N-methyl-D-aspartate glutamate receptors that highlights N-methyl-D\x=req-\
aspartate receptor hypofunction as a key mechanism that can help explain major clinical
and pathophysiological aspects of schizophrenia. The following fundamental features of
schizophrenia are accommodated by this hypothesis: (1) the occurrence of structural brain changes
during early development that have the potential for producing subsequent clinical manifesta-
tions of schizophrenia, (2) a quiescent period in infancy and adolescence before clinical manifes-
tations are expressed, (3) onset in early adulthood of psychotic symptoms, (4) involvement of do-
pamine (D2) receptors in some cases but not others that would explain why some but not all patients
are responsive to typical neuroleptic therapy, and (5) ongoing neurodegenerative changes and cog-
nitive deterioration in some patients. We propose that since N-methyl-D-aspartate receptor hypo-
function can cause psychosis in humans and corticolimbic neurodegenerative changes in the rat
brain, and since these changes are prevented by certain antipsychotic drugs, including atypical neu-
roleptic agents (clozapine, olanzapine, fluperlapine), a better understanding of the N-methyl-D\x=req-\
aspartate receptor hypofunction mechanism and ways of preventing its neurodegenerative conse-
quences in the rat brain may lead to improved pharmacotherapy in schizophrenia.
(Arch Gen Psychiatry. 1995;52:998-1007)
Despite a prodigious effort to clarify the
pathogenesis of schizophrenia, progress
has been modest and definitive answers to
the most pressing questions remain elu¬
sive. It is generally accepted that genetic
factors play an important role,1 but accu¬
mulating evidence from monozygotic twin
studies2 suggests that nongenetic prena¬
tal or perinatal factors may be powerful de¬
terminants of whether the schizophrenic
genotype will be expressed.
Accumulating evidence suggests that
structural changes in cerebrocortical and
limbic brain regions are characteristic of
the disorder,315 and there is a growing be¬
lief that these changes may originate in
utero.16"21 However, if the structural
changes occur in utero, why are symp¬
toms not expressed until early adult¬
hood?22 Why do some victims of the dis¬
ease show a progressive downhill course
suggestive of ongoing pathological brain
changes in adulthood? How can we ex¬
plain evidence23,24 suggesting that early
pharmacological intervention results in a
more favorable long-term outcome? Fi¬
nally, regardless of whether structural
changes originate in early life or adult¬
hood (or both), what is the mechanistic
basis for these structural changes, and how
do specific structural changes in diverse
anatomical locations relate to specific signs
and symptoms of the disease?
Several transmitter systems have been
implicated, but the primary focus has been
on the dopamine (DA) system25·26 be¬
cause of evidence that most drugs that
ameliorate psychotic symptoms in pa¬
tients with schizophrenia are DA recep¬
tor blockers. However, attempts to ex¬
plain the disease solely in terms of an
aberrantly overactive dopaminergic trans¬
mitter system leave many aspects of the
riddle unsolved.
Recently, it has been suggested that
combined dysfunction of the DA and glu¬
tamate (Glu) transmitter systems may be

St Louis, Mo.

 a mechanism worth considering. We
agree with this view, but we believe
that the concept, particularly with
respect to the potential role of Glu
receptor dysfunction, has not been
adequately developed. In this ar¬
ticle, we present a more fully devel¬
oped conceptual framework that is
empirically derived and that views
schizophrenia as a complex multi-
network disturbance in which hy¬
pofunction of a specific subtype of
Glu receptor, the N-methyl-D-
aspartate (NMDA) receptor, may
play a particularly important role.
NMDA RECEPTOR
HYPOFUNCTION HYPOTHESIS
Hypofunction of the Glu transmitter
system was first proposed as a causal
mechanism in schizophrenia by Kim
et al27based on theirfindingoflow con¬
centrationsofGlu incerebrospinalfluid
samples from schizophrenic patients.
Because subsequent attempts to con¬
firm this finding were notsuccessful,28
the Glu hypofunction hypothesis, as
originally formulated, did not gain
many adherents. However, several
See also pages 1008,
1011, 1015, and 1019
authors29"34 have generated new ver¬
sions of the hypothesis based largely
on the important discovery by Lodge
and colleagues35,36 thatphencyelidine,
apsychotomimeticdrugknown popu¬
larly as PCP or angel dust, noncom-
petitively blocks the ion channel of
the NMDA subtype of Glu receptor
(Figure I ). Recentevidence, reviewed
herein,strengthens thishypothesis and
justifies viewing NMDA receptor hy¬
pofunction (NRH) as a type of trans¬
mitter disturbance that, if present in
the brain of a schizophrenic patient,
could explain the followingmajor fea¬
tures ofthis disorder: (1) the full range
ofsymptoms (positive, negative, and
disorganized) thattypify the disorder;
(2) onset ofsymptoms in early adult¬
hood; (3) structuralbrainchanges; and
(4) cognitive deterioration.
NRH Is Psychotogenic
The NRH hypothesis, as initially for¬
mulated,29"31 held that since block¬
ade of NMDA receptor function by
phencyclidine is associated with
schizophrenia-like psychotic symp¬
toms, NRH per se may be psychoto-
genic and may be viewed as a candi-
date mechanism
to explain
schizophrenia. There now is new evi¬
dence confirming that NRH per se is
psychotogenic. Three competitive
NMDA antagonists (CPP, CPP-ene,
CGS 19755) that block NMDA re¬
ceptors by acting at the NMDA (rather
than phencyclidine) recognition site
(Figure 1) have now been adminis¬
tered to human subjects, and each was
found in low dosage to induce a
phencyclidine-like psychotic reac¬
tion.37"39 Therefore, the phenom¬
enon known for decades as "PCP psy-
chosis" is not a phencyclidine
receptor-specific phenomenon;
rather, it is a phenomenon that re¬
sults from effective blockade of the
NMDA receptor-ionophore com¬
plex (ie, effective induction of NRH).
Schizophrenomimetic Quality
of NRH-Induced Psychosis
Three decades ago, phencyclidine
was introduced into clinical medi¬
cine as an anesthetic agent, but it was
soon withdrawn because it caused
a high incidence ofpsychotic "emer¬
gence" reactions. Researchers in this
era were impressed by the schizo¬
phrenomimetic quality of the psy¬
chosis that was induced by phency¬
clidine in normal subjects; they were
also impressed with the ability of
phencyclidine to trigger a pro¬
longed recrudescence of the acute
psychotic state in stabilized chronic
schizophrenic patients.40"45 During
the past 30 years, illicit use of phen¬
cyclidine has resulted in countless
drug abusers being admitted to psy¬
chiatric hospitals with a psychotic
illness that is difficult to distin¬
guish from schizophrenia in acute,
subacute, or (sometimes) chronic
manifestations.46"48
Ketamine, a short-acting dis¬
sociative anesthetic agent with phen¬
cyclidine receptor-binding proper¬
ties, also produced emergence
reactions,49·50 but it gained accep¬
tance as an anesthetic agent in hu¬
man medicine because these unto¬
ward reactions could be suppressed
by benzodiazepines49·51 or pre¬
vented by barbiturates.52 The psy¬
chotic reaction induced by ket-

toms seen in patients with schizo-
phrenia.34·48·53"58
NRH Produces Brain Damage
In recent years, it has been shown
that various NMDA antagonists,
both competitive and noncompeti¬
tive, that cause psychotic reactions
in humans cause neurodegenera-
tive changes in corticolimbic re¬
gions of the rat brain.59"61 A single
low subcutaneous dose acutely in¬
jures neurons in the posterior cin-
gulate and retrosplenial (PC/RS) cor¬
tex; the injured neurons display a
vacuole reaction (Figure 2, B) de¬
tectable 4 hours after treatment, and
this reaction is followed by an ab¬
normal expression of 72-kd heat
shock protein that is most conspicu¬
ous 24 hours after treatment (Fig¬
ure 2, C) but remains detectable for
up to 2 weeks.60,62·63 A higher dose
causes an extensive heat shock pro¬
tein reaction (Figure 2, D to F) that
is followed over a 4-day period by a
neuron-necrotizing reaction that

Färber and colleagues87·88 have shown
that fetal rats and postnatal rats
younger than IV2 months (roughly
puberty in the rat) are totally insen¬
sitive to the cerebrocortical neuro¬
toxic action ofNMDA antagonists. Be¬
tween puberty (age, 1V2 months) and
full adulthood (ages, 3 and 4 months),
rats gradually become fully sensitive
to this toxic mechanism and remain
so until at least 10 months of age.
Similarly, the incidence of emer¬
gence reactions associated with ket-
amine-induced anesthesia is age-
occurring rarely, if ever, in prepuber-
tal children but manifesting in nearly
50% ofyoung to middle-aged adults.89
For this reason, ketamine is cur¬
rently used much more frequently in
pediatrie than adult medicine. Case
reports that have indicated a lack of
susceptibility of children to the psy-
chotic effects of phencyclidine90·91
suggest a similar age-dependency pro¬
file for this entire class of psychoto-
genic agents. Thus, rats have a gradual
onset of susceptibility to NRH-
induced neuronal injury in late ado¬
lescencejust as humans become sus¬
phencyclidinej-induced psychosis
and to the symptomatic expression of
schizophrenia in late adolescence.
It logically follows from the above age-
dependency information that a le¬
sion that has NRHpotential could be
present in the developing human
brain at birth, and this would not trig¬
ger psychopathological or neuro-
pathological processes until late ado¬
lescence, at which time it presumably
would spontaneously begin to trig¬
ger schizophrenia-like psychotic
symptoms. An in utero structural le-
sion that has a latent NRH psychoto-
genic potential that remains quies¬
cent until late adolescence would be
consistent with current specula¬
tion16"21 that in utero structural brain
changes occur in schizophrenia and
with the fact that psychotic symp¬
toms potentially attributable to such
structural changes do not begin to
manifest until late adolescence.22
is based on a disinhibition principle.
IfNMDA receptors in certain circuits
arehypofunctional, GABAergic inhibi¬
tory control over the activity of corti-
colimbic neurons is impaired. We
postulate that these circuits normally
mature and form their adult connec¬
tions in late adolescence. At this time,
if either the NMDA receptors or the
GABAergic neurons that possess these
receptors are missing or are impaired
(because of an earlier developmental
lesion), the newly formed circuits
wouldbe dysfunctional (uninhibited)
andwouldallowunmodulated stimu¬
latory activity to flood corticolimbic
brain regions—a process that could
produce psychotic symptomsinitially
and ongoingstructural changes even¬
tuallyifthe NRH conditionremains se¬
vere and unremitting. It is worth em¬
phasizing that the NRH condition
mightbeinducedduringdevelopment
by any mechanism that impairs the
functional status ofNMDA receptors
or that impairs or destroys specific

A long-standing concept in schizo¬
phrenia research is that a sensorimo-
tor gating disturbance may underlie
psychotic symptom formation. In
general, the concept is that an inhibi¬
tory filter or gating mechanism is op¬
erative in sensorimotor circuits that
normally prevents receptive centers
from being flooded with unmodu¬
lated information. For example, a fil¬
ter is presumed to be present at the
level of the thalamus that gates sen¬
sory inputs to the cortex. Recently,
Andreasen et al99 reported neuroim-
aging evidence of structural changes
in the thalamus ofschizophrenic pa¬
tients that might be viewed as mor¬
phological evidence for a damaged
thalamic filter. The NRH hypothesis
is consistent with this concept, but it
includes the added complexity that
several inputs to the cerebral cortex,
only one of which is thalamocorti-
cal, simultaneously lose their gating
competence (become disinhibited)
and flood cortical neurons conver-
gently via multiple-signal transduc-
tion pathways with unmodulated
stimulatory activity. Moreover, the
NRH mechanism is further compli¬
cated by failure of recurrent inhibi¬
tion at the cerebrocortical level.

In this article, we have advanced a uni¬
fied hypothesis that pertains to the
combined dysfunction of DA and
NMDA Glu receptors and that high¬
lights NRH as a key mechanism that
can help explain several fundamental
features ofschizophrenia. Thehypoth¬
esis derives credibility from evidence
schizophrenia-likepsychotic reactions
in adult humans and neuronal degen¬
eration in several cerebrocortical and
limbic brain regions in adult rats. We
postulate that NRH might be induced
in early human ontogenesis—perhaps
by an excitotoxic event; once induced,
it becomes a permanent condition in
the brain that can serve as a latent
mechanism that canbecome active and
spontaneouslytriggerpsychotic symp¬
toms andneurodegenerative changes.
However, the postulated NRH condi¬
tion cannottriggerneuropsychopatho-
logical effects until earlyadulthood in
viewofrecent evidence that, until early
adulthood, the brain is not sensitive to
the mechanismbywhich NRH induces
neuropsychopathological effects. The
NRH hypothesis of schizophrenia is
easilyintegrated with the DA hypoth¬
esis in that aprimary disturbance early
in life that causes hyperactivity of the
DAsystem is one possible mechanism
by which NRH might occur. Schizo¬
phrenia that is caused by this mecha¬
nism would have age of onset in early
adulthood andwould be responsive to
neuroleptic (DA antagonist) therapy,
whereas other forms ofschizophrenia
that involve NRH in the absence ofDA
hyperactivity would have the same
symptomatic manifestations and the
same age of onset but would be resis¬
tant to typicalneuroleptic therapy. We
conclude that the explanatory power
of the DA hypothesis of schizophre¬
nia is markedly enhancedbyfactoring
the NRH concept into the equation.
